Now, let's take a look at the evidence for voluntary commitments as influential amplifiers of the consistency principle. In this instance, within the healthcare arena, before medical conferences, organizers of business lunches and dinners between physicians and healthcare providers would call up the physicians and offer a free meal at an expensive restaurant. Then they'd send out a notice reminding doctors of the meeting. But to the organizers' frustration, about 50% of the doctors would cancel right before the scheduled meeting. To reduce the problem, a team of researchers used the stabilizing power of voluntary commitments in three ways. First, they had the meeting organizers let the doctors choose the restaurant they preferred from a list. Second, rather than specifying just one date for the meeting, they allowed the doctors to pick the day and time that worked best for them from a small list of options. Lastly, they sent a menu over in advance so the doctors could pre-select a drink, meal and dessert ahead of time. This set of independent choices made the doctors' commitments to the meeting and meal feel more voluntary. Consolations drop from 50% to 5%. The key insight for us in this example is that the more choices we allow others in selecting even the small details of an agreement, the more voluntary it becomes and consequently the more they'll live up to it.
